coredata.dc:title: The chain-like Au/carbon dots nanocomposites with peroxidase-like activity and their application for glucose detection
coredata.dc:creator: Gan, Hao
Han, Wenzhao
Fu, Zhendong
Wang, Liping
coredata.dc:description: The nanocomposites with highly synergistic effect show important potential application as nanozymes. Herein, the chain-like Au/carbon dots (CDs) (GCDs) nanocomposites were prepared by self-assembly method. The negatively charged Au nanoparticles (NPs) and positively charged CDs were connected by the electrostatic interaction. Then, the electron transfer between Au and CDs induces the strong catalytic effect of GCDs nanocomposites. The cross-linking reaction occurred between amino groups and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, which led to form the chain-like Au aggregation surrounded by carbon shells. By FDTD simulation, the aggregation of Au NPs may enhance the electromagnetic field so that the surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) signal can be increased based on GCDs nanocomposites as substrate. Otherwise, the GCDs nanocomposites can also be used to catalyze the oxidation of colourless3,3',5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxTMB in the presence of H2O2, which displays the enhanced peroxidase-like activity compared with alone Au NPs or CDs. The obvious oxidation process of TMB molecules may be monitored by the change of SERS signal during the catalytic reaction. On this basis, GCDs nanocomposites can be further used for detection of glucose. The detection level of glucose is obtained as low as 5 × 10−7 M. Therefore, this provides a method to detect the glucose based on GCDs nanocomposites as an enzyme mimic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GCDs
Chain-like
Peroxidase
Enzyme mimic
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Peroxidase-like catalytic activity of GCDs nanocomposites
The effect of experiment conditions on catalytic activity of GCDs
Glucose detection using GOx and GCDs nanocomposites
Synthesis and characterization of GCDs nanocomposites
The effect of aggregation of Au NPs induced by CDs on SERS enhancement
The effect of experiment conditions on catalytic activity of GCDs
GCDs nanocomposites as a peroxidase mimetic for colorimetric detection of glucose
Sensitivity and selectivity for glucose assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration for the synthesis of GCDs nanocomposites and the colorimetric detection of glucose based their peroxidase mimetic properties.

TEM images of (a) AuNPs; (b) CDs; (c) GCDs nanocomposites, and (d) HRTEM image of GCDs nanocomposites; (e) The zeta potential of Au NPs and CDs at different pH; (f) The FTIR spectrum of the GCDs nanocomposites.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of Au NPs, CDs, and GCDs nanocomposites; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of GCDs nanocomposites with different concentrations of Au NPs solution; (c) The fluorescence emission spectra of GCDs nanocomposites with different concentrations of Au NPs solution excited at 490 nm; (d) Energy band diagram of the Au NPs-CDs compound system.

Simulated enhancement factor of Raman scattering, the spaces between Au spheres are (a) 10 nm; (b) 2 nm; (c) 0 nm; (d) 0 nm. The Au spheres in (d) are covered by a 20nm-thick carbon shell. (e) SERS spectra of CDs and GCDs with different concentrations of Au NPs solution.

UV–vis absorption spectra of peroxidase-like activity in TMB and H2O2 with (a) CDs, (b) Au NPs and (c) GCDs during 25 min；The lineweaver-Burk Plot of the double reciprocal of Michaelis-Menten equation corresponding to CDs (d), Au NPs (e) and GCDs (f).

(a) The SERS spectra of TMB molecules at different time intervals during the peroxidase-like catalytic reaction process on the surface of the GCDs nanocomposites substrate in the presence of H2O2; (b) The UV–vis absorption spectra of GCDs nanocomposites toward the catalytic oxidation of H2O2/TMB system with the increasing of time; (c) The steady-state kinetic analysis using Michealis-Menten model for GCDs with the increasing of time; (d) EPR spectra of ·OH radicals in the presence of DMPO as a spin trap in the Au NPs/DMPO/H2O2, CDs/DMPO/H2O2, Au NPs-CDs/DMPO/H2O2systems, respectively.

Effect of (a) incubation time; (b) the concentration of TMB on the catalytic activity and (c) pH on the catalytic activity (note: The error bars stand for the 95 % confidence interval of the mean for absorbance responses and all the measurements were conducted repeatedly for six times).
(a) The mechanism of GCDs as a peroxidase mimetic for colorimetric detection of glucose；(b) A time-response curve for glucose detection using GCDs as catalytic agent.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB for the detection of glucose with different concentrations using GCDs nanocomposites as enzyme mimic. The inset was photographs of TMB with GCDs nanocomposites in the presence of different concentrations glucose under sunlight (left to right: 0 μM, 5 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM, 30 μM, 40 μM); (b) Linear relationship between absorption at 652 nm and glucose concentrations. (note: The error bars stand for the 95 % confidence interval of the mean for absorbance responses).

Selectivity analysis for glucose detection by monitoring the relative absorbance. The analyte concentrations were as follows: 5 mM fructose, 5 mM lactose, 5 mM maltose, and 0.8 mM glucose. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.

head.ce:title: The chain-like Au/carbon dots nanocomposites with peroxidase-like activity and their application for glucose detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Molecular Enzymology and Engineering, Ministry of Education, Jilin Universtiy, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory for Molecular Enzymology and Engineering, Ministry of Education, Jilin Universtiy, Changchun, 130012, China. Email:wanglp@jlu.edu.cn

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Molecular Enzymology and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nanocomposites with highly synergistic effect show important potential application as nanozymes. Herein, the chain-like Au/carbon dots (CDs) (GCDs) nanocomposites were prepared by self-assembly method. The negatively charged Au nanoparticles (NPs) and positively charged CDs were connected by the electrostatic interaction. Then, the electron transfer between Au and CDs induces the strong catalytic effect of GCDs nanocomposites. The cross-linking reaction occurred between amino groups and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, which led to form the chain-like Au aggregation surrounded by carbon shells. By FDTD simulation, the aggregation of Au NPs may enhance the electromagnetic field so that the surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) signal can be increased based on GCDs nanocomposites as substrate. Otherwise, the GCDs nanocomposites can also be used to catalyze the oxidation of colourless3,3',5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxTMB in the presence of H2O2, which displays the enhanced peroxidase-like activity compared with alone Au NPs or CDs. The obvious oxidation process of TMB molecules may be monitored by the change of SERS signal during the catalytic reaction. On this basis, GCDs nanocomposites can be further used for detection of glucose. The detection level of glucose is obtained as low as 5 × 10−7 M. Therefore, this provides a method to detect the glucose based on GCDs nanocomposites as an enzyme mimic.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, artificial enzyme mimics have been widely focused due to their similar functions with natural enzymes for the application in biotechnology, medical field, and chemical industry [1–5]. Natural enzymes are various and play a very important role in human body and life processes such as peroxidase, oxidase, etc [6]. However, inconvenient storage, difficulty of purification, instability and high cost restrict the application of natural enzymes [7–10]. Compared with natural enzymes, artificial enzyme shows more advantages including high stability, low lost and strong catalytic activity. Different kind of nanoparticles-based mimics have been designed to mimic natural enzyme including magnetic nanomaterials (Fe3O4 NPs and Co3O4 NPs) [11,12], carbon nanomaterials (carbon dots and graphene oxide) [13–15], noble metal NPs (Au NPs and Ag NPs) [16–19] and bimetallic nanocomposites (Au/Pt and Au/Pd) [20,21]. Especially, nanozymes based on their peroxidase-like properties attracted wide attention for application in many bio-detection.

To obtain nanozymes with high catalytic activity, nanocomposites with synergistic effect were focused due to their excellent multifunctional properties. CDs as unique optical nanomaterials have been extensively researched recently [22–28]. Here, CDs show strong adsorption with the metal nanoparticles due to their abundant surface ligands. Thus, CDs-based nanocompsites are easily to obtain by combining with other functional nanomaterials. Several groups have demonstrated that CDs/metal nanocomposites show enhanced catalytic activity [29,30]. The enhanced catalytic effect is due to the electron transfer from CDs to metals as reported previously [31]. Furthermore, metal nanoparticles may be assembled to form one-dimensional GCDs nanoparticles chain by the cross-linking reaction on the surface of CDs. The carbon shells on the Au NPs surface increased the surface area of GCDs nanocomposites, which may provide more active sites and improve the electron transfer efficiency than a single nuclear shell structure as mentioned above. Thus, the catalytic effect may be furthermore improved. Moreover, one-dimensional chain nanoparticles can exhibit stronger electromagnetic field than dispersed nanoparticles, which can be utilized for sensitive SERS detection [32–34].

Herein, chain-like CDs/Au nanocomposites were designed by self-assembling method. Au/CDs NPs were firstly connected by electrostatic interaction. Then the cross-linking reaction between amino groups and carboxyl groups on CDs occurred due to the catalytic effect of GCDs nanocomposites, which led to the formation of one dimensional chain-like nanocomposites. The GCDs nanocomposites exhibit admirable catalytic effect, which may be applied as nanozymes. GCDs nanocomposites could catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 to develop a blue color solution. SERS spectra may be used to investigate the catalytic mechanism of catalytic reactions. Therefore, this study provided a multifunctional GCDs nanocomposites as an enzyme mimic for detection of glucose.

Hydrogen tetrachloroauratetri hydrate (HAuCl4) and TMB were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Trisodium citrate, urea and p-phenylenediamine were obtained from Aladdin Reagents Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glucose, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and all other chemicals were obtained from Sangon (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals in the experiment were of analytical grade. All solutions were prepared with deionized water which was obtained through a Milli-Q water purification system.

Au NPs were synthesized by reducing HAuCl4 with trisodium citrate. In brief, 0.35 mL of 1% (w/v) trisodium citrate solution was quickly added to boiling 8 mL of 0.01 % (w/v) HAuCl4 solution under continuous stirring, with colour changes from pink to purple to the end of wine red. The CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. Initially, 0.12 g urea and 0.12 g p-phenylenediamine were added to 30 mL distilled water. Next, the autoclave filled with the mixed solution was heated at 160 ℃for 10 h. When it was cooled down to room temperature, the solution was filtered by 0.22 μm polyethersulfone membrane to remove large particles. Then, dark brown solution was obtained. Subsequently, the GCDs nanocomposites were synthesized by a self-assembly method. 1.5 mL of Au NPs colloids were added to 0.05 mL of CDs which was diluted to one third of its original concentration, adding ultrapure water to adjust the total volume to 4 mL. Finally, the resultant was shaken for 10 min and then incubated overnight at room temperature. The GCDs nanocomposites prepared here were stored at 4 ℃ for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like catalytic activity of GCDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like catalytic activity of GCDs nanocomposites was evaluated by following the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. Typically, 600 μL of TMB, 200 μL of H2O2 and 200 μL of GCDs nanocomposites were mixed together at room temperature. Peroxidase-like activity was measured by following the increase in absorbance at 652 nm for 20 min. The absorbance of above solution was recorded every 30 s. The oxidized TMB molecular signals were observed directly by measuring SERS spectra of the above mixing solution at different reaction times. Repeatability of the experiment for six times is confirmed based on the averaged values of the repeated measurements.

The catalytic kinetic experiments were performed by varying substrate concentration. Briefly, GCDs (58 μL) and TMB (200 μL, 106 cfu mL − 1) were first incubated at 37 °C for 40 min. The catalytic activity of GCDs on was studied by keeping the concentration of TMB and H2O2 at 200 mM. The absorbance of the solution at 652 nm was measured during 25 min. Similarly, the catalytic activity of GCDs and Au NPs was studied by keeping the concentration of TMB and H2O2. The catalytic kinetic parameters were calculated according to the Michaelis − Menten equation, V = Vmax × [S]/(Km + [S]), where v is the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, [S] is the concentration of substrate, and Km is the Michael is constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of experiment conditions on catalytic activity of GCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of incubation time, the concentration of TMB and pH on the peroxidase-mimetic activity of GCDs was evaluated. To confirm repeatability of the results, all the measurements were conducted repeatedly for six times. All the data points in the figures exhibits the averaged values of the repeated measurements. Herein, the effect of incubation time on peroxidase-mimetic is performed after preparing the sample for 100 min. Then The effect of concentration TMB on the catalytic activity has also been carried out. From 20–100 μM, the catalytic activity of GCDs is gradually increased. At higher concentration of TMB, GCDs exhibit strong catalytic effect. Finally, the effect of pH on catalytic activity has been measured from pH 5–11.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glucose detection using GOx and GCDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Glucose detection was achieved as follows: firstly, 20 μL of 10 mg/mL GOx and 100 μL of glucose with different concentrations were incubated at room temperature for 1 h. Then,600 μL of TMB, 300 μL of GCDs nanocomposites were added into the above glucose reaction. Next, the mixed solution was incubated at 37℃ for 10 min and analyzed at 652 nm of absorption spectra to determine the TMB conversion. Meanwhile, 5 mM fructose, 5 mM lactose and 5 mM maltose were chosen to verify the selectivity of glucose. Repeatability of the experiment for six times is confirmed based on the averaged values of the repeated measurements.

The morphologies of samples were characterized by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOLJEM-1200 EX) manipulating at 200 kV. The lattice spacing in high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image was determined using Digital Micrograph 3.5. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a Hitachi UH 4150 UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PTI-Quanta Master 400), using a Xenon lamp for excitation. The Raman spectra were obtained by the Lab RAM HR Evolution HR800 Raman microscope (HORIBA JobinYvon INC, France). The ESR spectra were monitored using Bruker EMXnano EPR (Electron Paramagnetic Resonance) spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected on a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GCDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The formation scheme of GCDs nanocomposites is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The chain-like Au/CDs nanocomposites were formed by self-assembly method. The electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged Au NPs and positively charged CDs make them attract to each other in acid environment. The TEM images show the formation of GCDs as shown in Fig. 2 (a–d). As shown in Fig. 2(a), pure Au NPs are spherical with good dispersion and their size is about 15 nm. Pure CDs is also good dispersion with the size about 5 nm as shown in Fig. 2(b). When the addition of CDs to Au NPs, the of Au NPs is aggregated, and CDs shells are formed on the surface of Au NPs shown in Fig. 2(c). The thickness of CDs is related with the amount of CDs. Fig. 2(d) exhibits obvious CDs about 20 nm as shown in Fig. 2(d). The attachment between Au NPs and CDs further is shown in Fig. 2(d). To prove that electrostatic interaction between positive and negative charges occurred during GCDs synthesis, the zeta potential of Au NPs and CDs was detected at different pH as shown in Fig. 2(e). The potential value decreases with increasing pH from a positive potential to a negative potential. The negatively charged Au NPs and positively charged CDs are connected by the electrostatic interaction at pH = 6. Therefore, the optimal pH of the reaction is 6. Then, in order to verify that the cross-linking reaction of the surface groups in the process of GCDs synthesis, the FTIR spectrum of GCDs was measured as shown in Fig. 2(f). The broad peak centered at 3420 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of NH, while the peak at 2950 cm −1 and 2820 cm−1 is related to the C–H stretching vibration. Moreover, the peaks at 1647 cm−1, 1694 cm−1, 1740 cm-1 are assigned to the CC, CN, C = O stretching vibrations. The broad peak at 1386 cm−1 is attributed to CN of acylamide of the Au NPs-CDs nanocomposites, indicating the formation of amino and carboxyl groups via cross-linking reaction [35,36].

During the formation of GCDs nanocomposites, the absorption spectra were further investigated for pure Au NPs, CDs and GCDs nanocomposites as shown in Fig. 3 (a). The absorption spectrum of CDs exhibits an absorption peak at 440 nm corresponding to the n−π* transition of CDs. The Au NPs present a characteristic peak at 520 nm. When addition of Au NPs into CDs solution, the absorption spectrum of GCDs nanocomposites appears a new shoulder peak around 720 nm. The appearance of new absorption peak is due to the plasmon coupling between Au NPs, which indicates that Au NPs and CDs are linked together. Then, to further study the relationship of CDs/Au NPs ratio with the structure of GCDs nanocomposites, different amount of Au NPs from 0.2 mL to 2 mL were injected into the same CDs solution, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the absorption peak intensity at 720 nm is enhanced with increasing the amount of Au NPs. It indicates that the plasmon resonance effect is more pronounced with increasing Au NPs concentration. Subsequently, the excitation wavelength of 490 nm is chosen to study the fluorescence performance of GCDs nanocomposites with different concentrations of Au NPs. Fig. 3(c) displays that the fluorescence intensity of GCDs nanocomposites gradually decreases with the addition of Au NPs. For confirm the change of fluorescence intensity, the energy level diagram is shown in Fig. 3(d). Because the Fermi level of Au (5.1 eV) is between the LUMO level and the HOMO level of the CDs, the electron transfer may occur from CDs to Au NPs. The flow of electrons from CDs into the Au Fermi level is allowed, leading to an increase in the electron density within the Au.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of aggregation of Au NPs induced by CDs on SERS enhancement
ce:section.ce:para: The electric field in the vicinity of Au NPs changes significantly with modulating their distances. Therefore, surface Raman scattering enhancement (SERS) may be modified accordingly [37]. Herein, a 3-D FDTD (Finite-Difference Time-Domain, Lumerical FDTD Solutions) method is used to calculate the enhancement factor, where is the electric field without Au spheres under different conditions. The wavelength of impinging light is set to be 488 nm, and the polarization is set to be along x direction. The refractive indices are got from Fig. 4 (a)–(d). When the Au spheres NPs are relatively far from each other, the electric field is enhanced individually. Basically there is no collective effects as shown in Fig. 4(a). When the distance between adjacent spheres is reduced to 2 nm, the hot spots in the small gaps may be observed as shown in Fig. 4(b). In this situation, we can expect highly SERS enhancement. If reducing the distance until the spheres contact with each other, the hot spots shrink and vanish at the touching points as shown in Fig. 4(c). As a result, the SERS enhancement decreases. Otherwise, the plasmonic properties of metal particles are also sensitive to the refractive index of surrounding medium. If the contacting Au NPs are covered by a 20 nm-thick carbon shell, the enhancement factor may further be reduced as shown in Fig. 4(d). Moreover, Raman active objects can only be put outside the carbon shell where the near-field electric field has already decayed. In this case, little SERS enhancement is left.

Based on theoretical calculation of the SERS enhancement effect, the SERS spectra of ATP were detected on CDs and GCDs nanocomposites with different concentrations Au NPs substrates. As shown in Fig. 4(e), the spectrum of the CDs shows weak SERS signal. Moreover, the SERS signals on the GCDs nanocomposites substrate exhibits significant enhancement, indicating that the GCDs nanocomposites possess higher SERS enhancement. As known, the complex of noble metal particles generally has a high Raman enhancement effect. It is generally believed that the enhancement of SERS can be attributed to the mechanism of electromagnetic enhancement and chemical enhancement. Electromagnetic enhancement is derived from the plasma resonance (SPR) of free electrons on the surface of metal nanoparticles (such as Au, Ag) in the UV–vis region. Under the surface plasmon resonance, the electromagnetic field in the vicinity of the metal nanoparticles can be greatly enhanced. So that the SERS enhancement of GCDs nanocomposites is mainly the electromagnetic enhancement induced by Au NPs. The excitation light causes the dynamic charge transfer between the energy level of the molecule and the energy levels of the metal particles. For GCDs nanocomposites, electron is transferred from CDs to Au NPs. Moreover, it can be seen that the intensity of SERS signal is gradually enhanced when the volume is increased from 0.5 mL to 1.5 mL. But when the volume continues to increase, the SERS intensity decreases. From the above results, it can be deduced that the proper increase in volume of Au NPs can lead to aggregate to a chain, enhancing the plasma resonance and local electric field. However, when the Au NPs is excessive, the distance between them is too close, reducing the emergence of hot spots. This is the main reason for the decrease of Raman signal intensity.

GCDs nanocomposites are not only an efficient SERS substrate, but also an excellent peroxidase-like catalyst. As shown in Fig. 5 , the typical absorbance peak of oxidation products of TMB at 652 nm increases during 25 min. In order to verify the catalytic efficiency of GCDs is higher than that of Au and CDS alone, we give the control group experiment. The system of Au NPs/TMB/H2O2, CDs/TMB/H2O2 were carried out. For Au NPs and CDs, the colorimetric process of TMB is lower than that of GCDs as shown in Fig. 5 (a–c). Obviously, GCDs nanocomposites exhibit mimetic enzyme properties. The catalytic efficiency may be calculated by the Michealis-Menten equation [38,39]:
1/V=(Km/Vmax)(1/[C])+(1/Vmax)
Where V and Vm respectively represent the initial and maximal reaction velocities, [C] represents the substrate concentration, and Km represents the Michaelis constant. The basic parameters can be obtained by using Linewearer-Burk double reciprocal plots as shown in Fig. 5 (d–f). The kinetic data is obtained from GCDs/TMB/H2O2 system. The catalytic effects by Vmax can be calculated as the reciprocal of the longitudinal intercept of the graph line. It can be obtained that the Vmax [GCDs] is about 0.1, the Vmax [Au NPs] = 0.0284 and Vmax[CDs] = 0.0091. Thus, the result demonstrates that Vmax[GCDs]＞Vmax[Au NPs]＞Vmax[CDs]. Therefore, GCDs is the best mimetic enzyme among the three systems by the comparison of maximum reaction rate. It is consistent with the absorption spectra.

Then, the oxidation process TMB was monitored by SERS spectra shown in Fig. 6 (a) in the presence of H2O2. It shows that the SERS signals of the TMB molecules oxidation by using GCDs nanocomposites as catalysts and SERS substrates at different time intervals. The typical bands at 1192, 1337 and 1611 cm−1 appear and the intensity of these bands increase with time, proving that TMB oxidation has carried out. During this process, GCDs nanocomposites can decompose H2O2 to hydroxyl radicals as peroxide enzymes, which can oxidize TMB into oxTMB. Therefore, GCDs /TMB/H2O2 system is further studied by UV–vis absorption spectra as shown in Fig. 6(b). The typical absorbance peak of oxTMB is located at 652 nm, which exhibited typical time-dependence increase. The catalytic effects by Vmax can be calculated as the reciprocal of the longitudinal intercept of the graph line as shown in Fig. 6(c). The spin trapping technique were used to demonstrate whether the catalytic activity of the nanozyme to decompose H2O2 and generate hydroxyl radical, which oxidize the substrate molecule. ESR spectroscopy was used to probe ·OH in the enzyme catalysis process. DMPO was used as spin trap matter that can react with ·OH to generate the stable DMPO/·OH spin adduct, which has a typical four lines ESR spectra with relative intensity being 1:2:2:1. Fig. 6d shows significantly higher signal intensity than CDs and Au NPs alone when measured under same condition. Obviously, GCDs nanocomposites exhibit higher mimetic enzyme properties than Au NPs and CDs. The improvement of peroxidase-like activity of GCDs could be attributed to the synergistic effect of Au NPs and CDs. Here, the aggregation of Au NPs induces the formation of chain-like GCDs nanocomposites, increasing the surface area to promote electron transfer. Therefore, catalytic effect of GCDs nanocomposites is enhanced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of experiment conditions on catalytic activity of GCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of incubation time, the concentration of TMB and pH on the peroxidase-mimetic activity of GCDs was evaluated as shown in Fig. 7 . To confirm repeatability of the results, all the measurements were conducted repeatedly for six times. All the data points in the figures exhibits the averaged values of the repeated measurements. Herein, the obtained sample of TMB+H2O2+GCDs exhibit obviously colorimetric process at room temperature. However, the colorimetric reaction may be decreased with increasing the incubation time at 40 °C. Therefore, the effect of incubation time on peroxidase-mimetic is obvious and the colorimetric reaction should be finished after preparing the sample. The effect of concentration TMB on the catalytic activity has also been investigated as shown in Fig. 7(b). From 20–100 μM, the catalytic activity of GCDs is gradually increased. At higher concentration of TMB, GCDs exhibit strong catalytic effect. Finally, the effect of pH on catalytic activity has been measured as shown in Fig. 7(c). The result shows that high catalytic activity may be performed from pH 5–6. When pH increases to alkaline, the catalytic activity decreased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GCDs nanocomposites as a peroxidase mimetic for colorimetric detection of glucose
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism for colorimetric detection of glucose can be described in Fig. 8 (a). Glucose and O2 produce gluconic acid and H2O2 under the action of GOx. Then, GCDs nanocomposites as a peroxidase mimetic can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to produce hydroxyl radicals, which can oxidize TMB to oxTMB. During this oxidation process, the solution color is changed to blue. Thus, a colorimetric method for the detection of glucose is developed. Herein, the change of absorption spectra at 652 nm was monitored in glucose/GCDs/TMB system during 25 min. As shown in Fig. 8B, the absorption intensity at 652 nm increases with increasing reaction time. It's the reason that H2O2 is generated during glucose oxidation, which can produce radicals to oxidize TMB. Repeatability of the experiment is confirmed based on the averaged values of the repeated measurements. It indicates that this method is effective for detecting glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity for glucose assay
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the applicability of the system, the sensitivity and selectivity for glucose detection were tested. Fig. 9(a) shows a typical glucose concentration response curve. With the increase of glucose concentration from 0 to 50 μM, the absorption intensity at 652 nm increases gradually. Then, the colorimetric photograph of TMB with different concentration of glucose (left to right: 0 μM, 5 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM, 30 μM, 40 μM) is obtained as shown in inset of Fig. 9(a). The color of mixture gradually changed from colorless to light blue to dark blue, which shows the concentration-dependence colorimetric properties. The detection level of glucose is obtained as low as 0.5 μM at a signal-to-noise raito of 3. Fig. 9(b) shows the linear relationship between absorption at 652 nm and glucose concentrations with the analysis obtained from Fig. 9(a). The regression equation is Abs = 0.02188 + 0.00455c (R2 = 0.99237). The results demonstrate that the absorption intensity is obviously related with the concentration of glucose. As the glucose concentration increases, the absorption peak at 652 nm gradually increases.

To validate the selectivity of this strategy for glucose detection, selective experiment for glucose is performed as shown in Fig. 10 . The control experiment displays that the absorbance negligibly increased for glucose analogues (fructose, lactose, and maltose) compared with glucose. Even when the control sample is used at concentrations as high as 5 mM, the signal is still very low. Only glucose exhibit obviously absorption change. Thus, the proposed method exhibits high selectivity for detecting glucose.

In summary, GCDs nanocomposites with a chain-like structure were synthesized by self-assembly method. With the addition of Au NPs, the fluorescence of GCDs nanocomposites were quenched. The enhancement of Raman signal of GCDs nanocomposites indicates that electromagnetic enhancement and charge transfer occurred. By using GCDs nanocomposites as catalysts and SERS substrates, the SERS signal of the TMB molecule can be clearly observed. GCDs nanocomposites exhibit high catalytic efficiency, which can catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to develop a blue color solution. It demonstrates that GCDs nanocomposites possess high peroxidase-like activity, which could be ascribed to the electron transfer enhancement. Based on the efficient peroxidase-like performance, GCDs nanocomposites can be used for colorimetric detection of glucose. The sensing platform provides a rapid, facile, and low-cost method for glucose monitoring.
Hao Gan: Complete the main experiment and writing- original draft preparation.
Wenzhao Han: Writing- reviewing and editing, data processing.
Zhendong Fu: Assist experiment.
Liping Wang: Tutor, guiding experimental design.

Author Contributions: Hao Gan and Liping Wang designed the experiments. Hao Gan carried out most of the experiments and wrote the manuscript. Wenzhao Han, Zhendong Fu performed some absorption and fluorescence spectral characterizations and discussed the experimental results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.31401086) and the Science and Technology Development Program of Jilin Province, China (No.20200404114YY).
